title: @johnennis: Here Comes the Judge (X Article)
rawContent:
Tweet by @johnennis (John Ennis)
Author bio: Math PhD, AI postdoc, author of @elves_skill
Text: [X Article link]
Article title: Here Comes the Judge
Article content: What's missing from agentic code review, and what we built to fix it.

Your app has a rule: when a customer cancels their subscription, they keep access until the end of their billing period. They paid for it. It's the right thing to do. It's also a promise buried in your business logic, not written down anywhere except the code itself.

Your agent refactors the subscription module overnight. The new code is cleaner. It passes every test. The review bot says it looks good. Types check. Lint is clean. The PR is green.

But in the refactor, the cancellation now takes effect immediately. The customer loses access the moment they cancel. No test catches this because no test was written for 'cancelled users retain access until period end.' The agent didn't know that was a promise. The review tool didn't know either. There's no bug. Everything works. The app just stopped doing what it's supposed to do.

This is intent drift. No tool on the market catches it.

Three levels of review:
1. Correctness — Is this code valid and well-written? (Solved by linters, type checkers, existing AI tools.)
2. Plan compliance — Does this code do what the plan said? (Elves does this already.)
3. Legality — Does the app still keep all its promises? (New concept — the gap being filled.)

The constitution: Every project maintains docs/constitution.md with deal-breaker behaviors. Contains flows (mermaid diagrams), business logic (pricing, eligibility, formulas), and invariants (things that must always be true). Not implementation details.

The judge: A read-only subagent that reads constitution + diff, traces flows/invariants through code, produces PASS/WARN/FAIL/UNCHANGED verdicts per intention. Any FAIL blocks the batch.

Bootstrapping: Agent proposes initial intentions from code; human reviews and edits. Human must own the constitution — if AI writes both code and success criteria, you've recreated the problem.

Flywheel: Constitution grows via planning (new features propose new intentions), mistakes (regressions become permanent safeguards), and incidents.

Regression attestation (v1.4.0): After each batch, agent reviews cumulative diff, identifies shared files touched, greps consumers, compares test baselines. Tests can only go up. Commits include 'Safe because:' line.
